primary_hypothesis: The West/East aggregate incident-rate disparity is fully explained in the supplied records by different severity mixes: West received a larger share of high-severity items, while West and East have the same incident rate within each recorded severity stratum.
rival_hypothesis: The West vendor staffing transition increased West's incident propensity relative to East within comparable severity strata, producing the aggregate disparity.
predicted_discrimination: A zero maximum within-severity West/East rate gap would support the severity-mix explanation and weigh against a staffing-related region effect within the recorded strata; a material nonzero gap, especially consistently higher in West, would weigh against a purely compositional explanation and support the rival. Mixed or small gaps would leave both explanations only partly resolved.
bounded_conclusion: For the supplied routed-item records and the two recorded severity strata, the regional aggregate disparity is accounted for by severity composition rather than a within-severity West excess. This does not establish causes beyond these records or rule out unmeasured differences within severity strata.
residual_uncertainty: Severity may be too coarse or inconsistently assigned, and unmeasured case-mix factors within the high- and low-severity strata could conceal a staffing-related effect; no pre-transition or otherwise time-resolved comparison is supplied.
recommended_consequence: Do not infer a basis for vendor sanction from the aggregate regional rates or this source pack. Validate severity assignment and routing practices, obtain time-resolved and finer case-mix data around the staffing transition, and escalate only if additional authorized review finds a persistent comparable-case disparity.
